content:
Provenance without intent is just archaeology.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The repository had twelve thousand lines merged last month. The author field on most commits said the same human name. The fingers that typed were mostly synthetic.
During an outage, someone asked a reasonable question: why does the notification service retry with exponential backoff capped at four seconds?
Nobody knew. The code comment said “standard retry policy.” The commit message said “add retry helper.” The pull request description said “agent implementation of spec v2.”
The spec v2 doc did not mention four seconds. There was no Architecture Decision Record. There was behavior, and there was regret.
Generated Code Arrives With Receipts, Not Reasons
When you type code yourself, intent leaks into the artifact even when documentation is lazy. You remember the argument in standup. You remember the Slack thread where someone said four seconds was enough because the vendor SLA was five.
When an agent types code, the reasoning evaporates unless you capture it deliberately. The diff is plausible. The reasoning is optional.
ADRs were always useful. In agent-heavy workflows they become load-bearing.
An ADR does not need ceremony. It needs:
→ Context: what problem and what constraints → Decision: what we chose → Consequences: what we traded away → Rejected options: what we did not choose and why → Reviewers: who signed off with production judgment
Without that, your architecture is a pile of confident defaults stitched together by a model optimizing for closure.
We started requiring ADR links on merges the week after the retry incident. Not because leadership loves documents. Because the cost of not knowing became visible on a customer-visible timeline.
Archaeology Is Expensive at 3 A.M.
The outage I mentioned was not dramatic. Retries hammered a rate-limited vendor. The queue backed up. Notifications lagged forty minutes. Customers noticed.
Fixing the cap was one line. Trusting the surrounding system took two days.
Engineers read code. They grep configs. They ask in channels where half the participants joined after the merge. Someone finds an old Loom link with bad audio. Someone else swears there was a doc that got deleted in a drive cleanup.
That is archaeology. It burns people who should be fixing forward.
An ADR would have taken ten minutes on merge day: “Cap at four seconds because vendor documents five-second SLA; lower cap risks duplicate sends on slow responses.” Debate it then. Cheap. Debate it during an incident. Expensive.
The on-call engineer told me later they spent ninety minutes proving the retry policy was intentional rather than accidental. Ninety minutes of human time to validate a four-second integer.
What Changes When Agents Author Most Diffs
Teams tell themselves they will document later. Later never wins against the next prompt.
Patterns that actually stick:
→ No merge on architectural choices without a linked ADR number in the PR → ADR template short enough to finish in one coffee, not one sprint → Agent prompts required to cite ADR constraints, not invent new ones silently → Quarterly ADR review that deletes decisions that stopped being true
Flat truth: most agents will happily introduce a new cache, a new queue, or a new retry policy because those are “best practices” in training data. Best practice without your context is someone else’s incident waiting for a host.
We now paste ADR excerpts into agent context windows before generation. The diff still arrives fast. The diff stops inventing parallel architectures.
ADRs Are Not Bureaucracy If You Keep Them Small
The failure mode of ADRs is volume theater. Nobody reads forty-page decision logs.
Good ADRs fit on one screen. They answer what a staff engineer would ask in review:
→ Why this datastore → Why sync versus async here → Why this failure mode is acceptable → What flag kills this path → What metric proves it unhealthy
Link the ADR in the PR. Link the PR in the ADR. Future you is a stranger. Future you deserves kindness.
Our template has five headings and a hard character limit. If you cannot explain the decision in that space, you probably have not decided yet.
Provenance Without Intent Fools Dashboards
Observability shows symptoms. ADRs explain accepted risks.
You can have green dashboards and latent bad decisions. Retry caps. TTL choices. Idempotency keys derived from timestamps. Feature flags defaulting on because the generator liked enabled defaults.
Code review catches syntax. ADRs catch strategy drift.
When review becomes “does this look idiomatic,” idiomatic wins over correct-for-us. ADRs force the second conversation.
During a recent game day we injected latency into a dependency. Metrics moved. On-call triaged quickly because the ADR for that integration listed acceptable degradation and the flag that isolates the blast radius. No Loom archaeology required.
How to Start Without a Program Office
Pick one service. Require ADRs for:
→ New external dependency → New persistence format → New retry or timeout policy → New auth scope → Anything an on-call person would need to reason about under stress
Train the agent workflow: spec references ADR draft, code references ADR number, reviewer checks alignment.
In two months you will wonder how you operated without them. Not because paperwork is fun. Because incidents got shorter.
Start with decisions you already regret not writing down. Name the retry policy. Name the cache TTL. Name why the feature flag defaults the way it does. Retroactive ADRs feel awkward and still pay rent the first time production asks why.
Linking ADRs to Agent Workflows Without Slowing Shipping
The fear is paperwork kills velocity. The reality on our team was the opposite once ADRs became prompt inputs rather than post-merge homework.
Morning workflow now looks like this: engineer drafts a one-screen ADR for anything that touches persistence, retries, or external vendors. Reviewer approves or edits in ten minutes. Agent session starts with ADR text pinned. PR template requires ADR number. Reviewer checks alignment, not vibes.
When the agent proposes a new queue because “best practice,” the ADR either already authorized that tradeoff or the diff gets rejected with a link to update the ADR first. That loop sounds heavy until you compare it to forty minutes of grep during an outage.
We also index ADRs in the repo search the same way we index runbooks. On-call can type “retry notification vendor” and land on intent, not only on implementation. That single search improvement cut median triage time on notification incidents measurably last quarter.
Agents did not replace ADRs. They made missing ADRs unbearable because generated code arrives faster than human memory fails.
The Review Question That Caught Three Drifts Last Month
Our staff review template now includes one line: “Which ADR governs this diff, and what did it reject?” Empty answers get sent back without line comments.
Three recent catches from that line alone:
→ An agent added Redis caching to a path ADR marked sync-only for audit reasons → A timeout change violated vendor contract language documented in ADR-118 → A new webhook consumer appeared though ADR-44 explicitly deferred async ingestion
None were malicious. All were plausible. All would have become archaeology within two sprints.
Flat dry truth: if your review culture optimizes for idiomatic code, agents will win every time. If your review culture optimizes for documented intent, agents become fast implementers instead of stealth architects.
On-Call Quotes That Convinced Leadership
During a severity-two, someone asked in chat: “Did we mean to retry forever here?” Silence. Then twenty minutes of archaeology.
I forwarded that chat thread to leadership with one ADR draft attached showing how ten minutes of merge-day writing would have answered the question instantly. We got ADR enforcement not because I love paperwork, but because the alternative has a measurable half-hour tax per ambiguity.
Another quote: “The comment says standard policy. Whose standard?” That one became a template footer: rejected options required, even when the agent suggests only one path.
Small documents. Large incident savings. Flat voice because the math is flat.
Provenance without intent is archaeology. Intent captured early is infrastructure.
What Belongs in the ADR Now
Write the invariant the agent cannot be trusted to infer.
→ Why this boundary exists → What must never happen even if tests are green → Which rollback is real versus theatrical → Which metric defines success for humans, not for demos → Who owns the decision when the generated code disagrees with the doc
If the ADR is only a summary of what was built, it is a press release. If it constrains what may be built next, it is infrastructure for a team that generates faster than it remembers.
When you need vocabulary for consequences — not buzzwords — before the next agent merge lands, The Production Engineering Library: The Complete Collection for Production Systems, System Design & Backend Engineering is the reference set I open when writing ADRs under time pressure.
Froquiz helps you practice defending tradeoffs out loud: 10,000+ questions across SQL, Docker, Git, AWS, Java, Python, JavaScript, React, microservices, including Senior Dev Challenge scenarios where you pick a path and explain what you gave up.
→ Froquiz
